score_summary: The opportunity remains real but the window is narrowing. CAPE has not yet launched, 94% of importers are still unenrolled, and the legal foundation is solid. However, competition is building fast and fee compression is likely once CAPE goes live. The moderate scenario ($8-10M net) is achievable if you move within the next 30 days.

factors.name: CAPE System Friction
factors.reasoning: CAPE has not launched as of mid-April 2026. The March 31 filing showed mass processing at only 60% complete. Importers cannot yet self-file, preserving strong intermediary demand.

factors.name: Time Window
factors.reasoning: 53 million entries remain in the backlog. Even after CAPE launches, CBP estimates 45 days per claim. At scale, processing will take 6-12 months minimum.

factors.name: Importer Unpreparedness
factors.reasoning: Only ~21,000 of 330,000+ importers have registered for electronic deposits. 12,300+ approved refunds are stuck in rejected status. The enrollment gap is widening weekly.

factors.name: Legal Stability
factors.reasoning: Supreme Court ruling is final and unambiguous. The government's May 4 appeal deadline on finally-liquidated entries is the main risk, but core refund authority is settled.

factors.name: Competitive Space
factors.reasoning: TRG, Flexport, McCune, and multiple law firms are active. The mid-market segment ($10K-$500K refunds) is less contested but filling up. First-mover advantage is eroding.

factors.name: Fee Sustainability
factors.reasoning: Law firms charge 15-20%, but broker-level services are converging on 3-5%. Free tools from Flexport add downward pressure. Fees will compress further once CAPE launches.

factors.name: Regulatory Risk
factors.reasoning: Section 122 replacement tariff expires July 24 unless Congress extends. 24 states challenging it adds uncertainty. No direct threat to IEEPA refunds but regulatory environment is volatile.
